0: Topic T0 gathers work on the coordination of port calls and the inter-organizational information exchange that underpins them, as reflected in its leading terms (port_call, information_sharing, barrier, berth, arrival, coordinate). Its dominant articles centre on how digital systems align the actions of shipping companies, terminals and port managers around vessel arrivals, ranging from Just-in-Time arrival and berth allocation policies (e.g., Mubder, 2024; Pahl, 2024) to the mechanisms sustaining continued use of Port Community Systems (e.g., Mubder and Fredriksson, 2025) and the effect of port-centric information integration on performance (Jiang et al., 2023). What unites these studies is a process-oriented view of digitalization: technology matters insofar as it enables timely, shared information among the many actors involved in a single port call, while institutional and organizational barriers condition whether that coordination materializes (Gustafsson, 2007; Fedi et al., 2019). With eleven dominant articles spanning 2007 to 2025, this constitutes a mid-sized but core stream of the corpus, bridging the operational literature on berth and arrival management with the broader Port Community System adoption debate.
1: Anchored by terms such as attack, threat, vulnerability, detection, and cybersecurity alongside safety, compliance, and regulatory, T1 captures the research stream concerned with securing and safeguarding the digital infrastructure on which port and maritime operations increasingly depend. Its most strongly loading documents address cyber risk directly, from a discrete-event-systems survey of maritime and port cybersecurity (e.g., Liu et al., 2025) to threat and security models for cross-border paperless trade systems (Kayisoglu et al., 2025) and an early investment analysis of information security management in Croatian seaports (Aksentijević et al., 2012). The remaining articles broaden the frame from deliberate attacks to the dependability and regulatory soundness of digital infrastructure more generally, spanning container-handling systems, customs legal frameworks, and maritime logbooks as strategic infrastructure. What unites the cluster is a shared view of digitalization as a source of exposure as well as capability, treating risk assessment, incident detection, and compliance as prerequisites for trustworthy port systems. Within the corpus this is a secondary but clearly delineated stream rather than a core one, and its concentration of very recent publications suggests a theme that is gaining momentum as ports' digital dependence deepens.
2: A smaller, methods-oriented cluster, T2 gathers quantitative modeling work in which digitalization is examined through its effects on port operational performance, resilience, and service quality, as reflected in top terms such as optimization, modeling, variable, berth, disruption, and container_terminal. One strand comprises formal optimization and simulation frameworks — an event-driven real-time tactical-operational optimization framework for smart ports (e.g., Abril et al., 2024), a computational Port Community System framework for synchromodal freight networks (e.g., Nabais et al., 2018), and resilient design of hyperconnected Physical Internet supply networks (Tordecilla et al., 2025) — while a second strand uses construct-based survey models linking information systems use and digital adoption to perceived service quality and business sustainability, notably in Indonesian ports (e.g., Bakari and Subriadi, 2020; Utama et al., 2024), which explains the prominence of the indonesia term. What unites these otherwise methodologically diverse papers is a shared analytical posture: digital systems are treated as measurable inputs to models of port efficiency, resilience to disruption, and service outcomes rather than as institutional or governance phenomena. With only a handful of dominant documents and a modest share of the corpus, this is a niche, somewhat heterogeneous stream at the margins of the collection, complementing the larger adoption- and governance-focused topics with a performance-modeling perspective.
3: Topic T3 captures the socio-organizational side of port digitalization: research on the barriers, stakeholder dynamics, and maturity of digital innovation adoption, as signalled by top terms such as barrier, terminal_operator, ict, edi, success, power, and maturity_model. Its dominant articles examine why inter-organizational systems succeed or stall, ranging from barriers and facilitators of digital innovation and cost-benefit framings of PCS competitiveness (e.g., Carlan et al., 2016, 2017) to knowledge-management obstacles in PCS-enabled ports (Zerbino et al., 2018) and maturity models for smart ports (Boullauazan et al., 2023). What unites the cluster is a shift of analytical focus from the technology itself to the actors around it — terminal operators, shipping lines, and shippers — whose interests, power relations, and political dynamics shape adoption, a thread made explicit in studies of de-politicization at Tema (Aryee & Hansen, 2022) and of power mutation in Piraeus (Makris & Vradis, 2024). Within the corpus this constitutes a mid-sized but clearly delineated core stream, providing the behavioural and institutional counterpart to the more technical architecture- and platform-oriented topics.
4: Rather than a substantive application domain, topic T4 gathers the corpus's meta-scientific layer: bibliometric analyses, systematic reviews, and research-trend mapping of port digitalization, as signalled by top terms such as "publication," "cluster," "trend," "academic," "theme," and "systematic" alongside the technology buzzwords ("big_data," "iot," "smart_port," "sensor") that these studies track. Its dominant documents survey the field itself rather than a particular system, ranging from analyses of maritime technology attention cycles and buzzword stability (e.g., Jalali & Tei, 2025) to a structured literature review of Port Community Systems (Moros-Daza et al., 2020) and a systematic review of artificial intelligence in seaport smart-gate processes (Teixeira et al., 2026). What unites these articles is a shared review methodology — searching, clustering, and thematizing prior publications to chart where scholarly attention on smart ports and maritime logistics is heading — rather than a common empirical object. With only four dominant articles and a modest share of corpus attention, T4 is a small, clearly niche cluster, and it is somewhat heterogeneous at the margins (Lai, 2025, on data-fusion-driven collaborative logistics, sits less squarely in the review genre); nonetheless, it plays a useful reflexive role by consolidating and orienting the substantive streams captured by the other topics.
5: Sharply delineated by its vocabulary, this cluster captures the literature on distributed-ledger technology as an alternative or complement to conventional port community systems for handling trade documentation: the dominant terms (blockchain, smart_contract, node, bill_lading, ethereum, and the TradeLens platform) point to technically oriented work on decentralized architectures for document exchange among sellers, freight forwarders, and port actors. Representative contributions include architectural design requirements for a blockchain-based PCS (e.g., Irannezhad, 2020), the use of IoT and blockchain to address bill-of-lading issues (Irannezhad and Faroqi, 2023), and a conceptual comparison of PCS and blockchain scenarios for maritime document handling (Tsiulin et al., 2020), alongside proposals extending the technology to interport communities (Serra et al., 2022). What unites these articles is a shared focus on trust, disintermediation, and smart-contract automation in cross-organizational documentary flows, typically at the level of design proposals, prototypes, and feasibility assessments rather than large-scale deployments. Within the corpus this is a comparatively small but highly coherent and technology-specific stream, reflecting a wave of blockchain enthusiasm concentrated around 2020-2023 rather than a core pillar of the port-digitalization literature.
6: Topic T6 captures the design and national implementation of maritime single window environments and their integration with Port Community Systems, as signalled by terms such as nmsw, nsw, module, imo, reporting, clearance, and vts. Its dominant articles are largely case-based accounts of building these platforms in specific national contexts, particularly Croatia and Montenegro (e.g., Tijan et al., 2018; Kapidani et al., 2021; Aksentijevic et al., 2022), alongside taxonomic and architectural contributions such as Rødseth and Kapidani (2017) and Hagaseth et al. (2023). What unites these studies is a system-engineering and implementation perspective: they specify modular architectures, prototypes, risk-management approaches, and cost-benefit assessments for harmonizing ship reporting formalities and administrative clearance through a single national entry point, often under IMO and EU regulatory impetus. The strong presence of Adriatic and small-coastal-state cases (Rijeka, Split, Bar, Taranto) indicates that this stream is heavily shaped by a regional research community documenting successive stages of the same digitalization programmes. With one of the largest sets of dominant documents in the corpus, T6 constitutes a core stream, representing the institutional-infrastructure backbone of seaport digitalization research.
7: Anchored by terms such as "msw", "trade_facilitation", "agency", "administration", and country markers like "montenegro", "turkey", and "india", this stream gathers country-level case studies of maritime single window implementation as an instrument of trade facilitation and administrative reform (e.g., Beškovnik, 2015; Keceli, 2011; Peynirci, 2023). Its documents typically examine how national administrations and port communities in specific settings—the Adriatic region, Turkey, Montenegro's Port of Bar, India, and Indonesia (Yusuf, 2023)—introduce single-window platforms to streamline document submission across agencies, reduce clearance times, and align with international trade law. What unites them is a policy-and-implementation orientation: rather than proposing generic architectures, they evaluate concrete national deployments and their institutional prerequisites, with the terms "reduction", "hour", and "estimate" reflecting a recurring concern with quantified efficiency gains. The cluster is nonetheless somewhat heterogeneous at its margins, absorbing data-space and cognitive-platform contributions (e.g., Sarabia-Jácome et al., 2020; Belsa et al., 2023) that share the emphasis on inter-agency data exchange. With a dominant-article count and prevalence close to the corpus average, it constitutes a solid mid-sized stream that complements the more architecture-focused single-window topic (T6) with an implementation and trade-policy perspective.
8: Anchored by the corpus's most PCS-specific vocabulary (pcs, export, shipment, import, custom_declaration, freight_forwarder, portbase), Topic T8 captures the operational core of Port Community System research: how electronic platforms streamline import/export documentation and cargo information flows across the port logistics chain. Its dominant articles concentrate on efficiency and process improvement in concrete port settings, from integrated information flows and service-oriented PCS modeling (e.g., Bisogno et al., 2015; Nota et al., 2018) to the effect of PCS characteristics on port performance (Caldeirinha et al., 2020) and group-specific process improvements in Rotterdam (Simoni et al., 2022), with a notable Italian and broader European case orientation. What unites these studies is a shared framing of the PCS as a documentary and logistical hub whose value is measured in reduced processing times and smoother coordination among forwarders, customs and terminal actors, extending more recently toward Physical Internet and track-and-trace architectures (Fahim et al., 2021). Spanning 2009 to 2025 and accounting for one of the largest shares of the corpus, this is unambiguously a core stream — arguably the thematic backbone of the PCS literature reviewed here.
9: Topic T9 gathers the evaluative and decision-analytic strand of smart-port research, in which digitalization is assessed through indicators, key performance indicators, composite indices and expert-weighted criteria (terms such as smart_port, indicator, kpi, evaluation, ahp, expert). Its dominant articles build measurement frameworks and prioritization models: performance evaluation of container terminal operation systems (e.g., Liu et al., 2022), identification of key success factors for smart ports in the post-pandemic era (Hsu et al., 2023), optimal smart-technology adoption strategies under network externalities (Li et al., 2023), and multi-criteria ranking of resilience drivers in Chilean ports (González-Solano et al., 2025). What unites these studies is a shared methodological orientation — multi-criteria decision-making, expert elicitation and dashboard- or index-based assessment — applied to the question of how "smart" a port is and which investments or strategies should be prioritized. That the stream reaches back to early performance evaluation of Port Community System implementation (Tsamboulas et al., 2012) shows continuity between older PCS appraisal work and current smart-port benchmarking. Within the corpus this constitutes a coherent mid-sized stream rather than a core one: smaller than the dominant PCS and governance topics, but clearly delineated by its quantitative-evaluative focus.
10: Anchored by the terms governance, sustainability, digital_transformation, ecosystem, and public_private, this stream captures research that frames port digitalization as an institutional and strategic transformation rather than a purely technical one. Its dominant articles range from studies of port authority reform and the governance of inter-organizational information sharing (e.g., de Langen & van der Lugt, 2017; Klievink et al., 2012; Tijan et al., 2021) to assessment frameworks for digital maturity, smart-port indices, and port sustainability (e.g., Franchi et al., 2025; Molavi et al., 2020). What unites these contributions is a management- and policy-level lens: digital transformation is analyzed through the governance capacity of port authorities, public-private collaboration, and its alignment with sustainability and energy-transition objectives, frequently operationalized via maturity models, indicators, and comparative regional assessments. As the largest cluster in the corpus, it constitutes the conceptual core of the field, linking the more technically oriented topics on platforms, single windows, and smart-port technologies to broader questions of institutional design and sustainable development.
11: Occupying the margins of the corpus, T11 captures a small, methodologically distinctive stream that applies taxonomy development to digital-platform business models in freight transport, as signalled by the dominant terms taxonomy, business_model, dimension, cluster, and digital_platform alongside marketplace, value_creation, and governance. Its two dominant articles—Bossong et al. (2025) on emerging business models of digital platforms in intermodal freight transport, and Teßmann and Elbert (2022) on a taxonomy of multi-sided B2B platforms under varying governmental influence—share a common design-science logic: classifying platform ventures along dimensions such as value proposition, geographic scope, profit orientation, and governance arrangements. What unites them is the treatment of port community systems and freight marketplaces not as IT artefacts but as platform businesses whose ecosystems and value-creation mechanisms can be systematically typologized. With only two dominant documents and a marginal share of the corpus, this is clearly a niche, borderline residual cluster rather than a core research stream, yet it usefully connects the port digitalization literature to the broader platform-economy and business-model scholarship.
